mnemonics.title: CPU Scheduling Criteria Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: CUT-WAR FORMULA: C-U-T = CPU Utilization, Throughput | W-A-R = Waiting time, Average waiting time, Response time | FORMULA = Fairness, Optimization, Response, Load balancing, Memory management, Utilization, Fairness (actually just remember: UTW+RF - Utilization Throughput Waiting Response Fairness)

mnemonics.title: Remember Turnaround Components
mnemonics.content: TURN AROUND = Time in Ready queue + UNinterrupted execution + And all I/O time + Response + Departure time - Actually simple: TAT = Waiting Time + Burst Time + I/O Time

acronyms.expansion: Central Processing Unit - The component that executes instructions
acronyms.usage: When measuring CPU Utilization, we track how busy this unit is

acronyms.expansion: Turnaround Time - Completion time minus arrival time
acronyms.usage: TAT = Completion Time - Arrival Time = Waiting Time + Burst Time + I/O Time

acronyms.expansion: Waiting Time - Total time in ready queue
acronyms.usage: WT = Turnaround Time - Burst Time - I/O Time

acronyms.expansion: Shortest Job First - Scheduling algorithm that minimizes average waiting time
acronyms.usage: SJF selects the process with smallest burst time next

acronyms.expansion: First Come First Serve - Simplest scheduling algorithm
acronyms.usage: FCFS processes arrive in order, no prioritization
RR gives each process equal time slices, good for response time

acronyms.expansion: Multi-Level Queue Scheduling - Multiple queues for different process types
acronyms.usage: MCS separates processes by type (system, interactive, batch)

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: |---Waiting---|---Execution---|---I/O---| Total length = Turnaround Time. The waiting segment is what schedulers try to minimize.
Think of a wave pattern: CPU-Burst → I/O Burst → CPU-Burst → I/O Burst. Processes oscillate between these states. CPU scheduler activates at each CPU-burst start.

Balance scale: On one side EFFICIENCY (shortest jobs first, maximize throughput), on other side FAIRNESS (all jobs get CPU). SJF improves efficiency but may cause starvation - scale tips away from fairness.
User types command → finger stops → screen shows response. The gap between finger-stop and screen-response is Response Time. This gap must be small for good user experience.

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant kitchen: CPU Utilization = percentage of time the chef is actually cooking vs standing idle. A busy restaurant (high utilization) is financially better.
Like a highway toll booth: Throughput = number of cars passing through per hour. More cars processed = higher throughput = better road usage.
Like a dry cleaning service: You drop off clothes (arrive) and pick them up (complete). Total time from drop-off to pickup is turnaround time.
Like waiting in line at a bank: You sit in the queue waiting for your turn. The time actually spent waiting in line (not being served) is waiting time.
Like a live chat support: You send a message and wait for first reply. The time until first reply appears is response time - crucial for satisfaction.
Like a teacher calling on students: Fair teacher ensures all students eventually get called, not always picking the quickest answers (which would be efficient but unfair).
